study: Hertz, Jayasundera, Piraino, Selcuk, Smith, and Verashchagina (2008), The Inheritance of Educational Inequality
data: Cross-country educational attainment data over roughly 50 years
core_relevance: benchmark global evidence on persistence and long-run cross-country variation
note: Useful for motivating why Uzbekistan should be placed in a broader comparative mobility frame.

study: Causa and Johansson (2010), Intergenerational Social Mobility in OECD Countries
core_relevance: links educational persistence to policy and opportunity structures
note: Useful for linking mobility to broader growth and policy questions.

study: Torche (2015), Intergenerational Mobility and Equality of Opportunity
data: Review of sociological and economic mobility literature
core_relevance: explains what mobility measures do and do not identify
note: Key source for framing the paper as descriptive of intergenerational association rather than a full equality-of-opportunity test.

study: van der Weide, Lakner, Mahler, Narayan, and Gupta (2024), Intergenerational Mobility around the World: A New Database
core_relevance: most comprehensive recent education mobility database
note: Supports the claim that low-income settings tend to have lower educational mobility and provides methodological benchmarks.

study: Strömberg and Engzell (2025), How Robust Are Country Rankings in Educational Mobility?
core_relevance: shows educational mobility rankings are sensitive to coding and measurement choices
note: Direct justification for using a compact set of multiple mobility measures rather than relying on one ranking.

study: Verashchagina (2009/2012), Education and Socioeconomic Mobility in Post-Communist Countries
core_relevance: places educational mobility in former Soviet and Eastern European settings
note: Useful for showing that transition settings do not follow one simple mobility path.

study: Brück and Esenaliev (2018), Post-socialist Transition and Intergenerational Educational Mobility in Kyrgyzstan
core_relevance: closest Central Asian comparator for long-run mobility in a transition economy
note: Important comparator because it combines Soviet legacy, transition, and Central Asian context.

study: Aydemir and Yazici (2019), Intergenerational Education Mobility and the Level of Development: Evidence from Turkey
data: National survey with parent-child schooling information
core_relevance: regional comparator in a middle-income setting with large spatial disparities
note: Useful for discussing how development, region, and place interact with schooling mobility.

study: Akarçay-Gürbüz and Polat (2017), Schooling Opportunities and Intergenerational Educational Mobility in Turkey
data: 1990 and 2000 censuses plus IV using historical enrolment rates
core_relevance: shows how a causal paper would need exogenous region-time variation
note: Useful for design-positioning: contrasts with the current paper’s descriptive scope.

study: World Bank (2018), Uzbekistan Education Sector Analysis
data: Sector analysis using administrative and comparative education data
core_relevance: documents low tertiary access and demand-access mismatch before rapid post-2017 expansion
note: Foundational context for why educational mobility is salient in Uzbekistan.

core_relevance: updates sector reforms and access concerns
note: Good bridge between pre-reform structure and recent reform-era expansion.

study: World Bank (2024), Country Gender Assessment: Uzbekistan
core_relevance: supports gendered interpretation of education and labor-market pathways
note: Use selectively in subgroup and interpretation discussion rather than as a central mobility source.

study: UNICEF (2023), Situation Analysis of Children and Adolescents in Uzbekistan
data: National child and adolescent situation analysis
core_relevance: documents rural-urban disparities, migration pressures, and unequal access to services
note: Useful for contextualizing region and household correlates.

study: Alam and Tiwari / UNICEF (2020), Putting the 'learning' back in remote learning
data: Policy issue brief synthesizing global evidence
core_relevance: summarizes why remote learning shifts burden to parents and caregivers
note: Good general background for Module C; not a source for Uzbekistan-specific claims.

study: Muñoz-Najar et al. / World Bank (2021), Remote Learning During COVID-19: Lessons from Today, Principles for Tomorrow
data: Synthesis of remote-learning practice and evidence
core_relevance: highlights teacher-parent communication and home-learning support
note: Useful for connecting household support to continuity of learning.

study: Daniela et al. (2021), Parents’ Perspectives on Remote Learning in the Pandemic Context
core_relevance: shows parents became learning agents and needed school support
note: Helpful as supporting evidence, but not essential.

study: Knopik et al. (2021), Parental involvement in remote learning during the COVID-19 pandemic
core_relevance: documents variation in parental involvement and barriers during remote education
note: Useful for broadening the parent-support discussion.

study: Iqbal, Patrinos, and coauthors (2025), Learning during the pandemic: Evidence from Uzbekistan
data: Student assessments in 2019 and 2021 plus questionnaires
core_relevance: Uzbekistan-specific pandemic evidence with heterogeneity by parental education and device access
note: Use carefully as bounded background; the current paper does not estimate learning loss.

study: Meliboeva, Patrinos, and Teixeira (2020), Uzbekistan: A timely response on learning during school closures
data: World Bank blog summarizing policy response
core_relevance: documents TV-based and online emergency response and equity concerns
note: Useful descriptive background for the policy environment during school closures.
